title: 英特尔前高管谈英特尔如何在从内存芯片转向CPU后在计算机行业取得主导地位，然后随着智能手机的出现而失去主导地位
title_en: Former Intel executive on how Intel achieved dominance in the computer industry after pivoting from memory chips to CPUs and then lost it as smartphones emerged

note:
想象一下这个感恩节：土耳其，足球(也许)，与亲戚的互动比往常更紧张。也许还有一个新的传统：找出谁真正赢得了总统选举。
冠状病毒危机意味着，宾夕法尼亚州等州可能会连续数周清点邮寄选票，而特朗普总统则在推特上发布关于欺诈的虚假指控。美国民主和深度政治危机之间的最后障碍可能是电视新闻和纽约时报网站上某种版本的令人抓狂的针头。
上周，我与美国主要新闻编辑部的高管、电视主持人和选举分析师进行了交谈，一些高级经理和主持人的乐观信心给我留下了深刻的印象，他们通常表示，他们以前处理过复杂的选举，现在还可以这样做。我对一些最关注的人近乎恐慌感到震惊-分析人士和制片人试图从州选举官员那里获得关于他们将如何以及何时计票和报告结果的答案，但往往以失败告终。
“书呆子们都吓坏了，”决策台总部(Decision Desk HQ)的创始人布兰登·芬尼根(Brandon Finnigan)说。该公司向媒体发布选举结果。“我不认为这在普通观众的脑海中有足够的穿透力，不会有选举之夜。通常情况下，小组坐在一起讨论选举结果的喧闹场面--那是死气沉沉的，“他说。
首先，已经发生了从单日面对面选举的转变。在2018年中期选举中，只有60%的选票是在选举日亲自投票的。今年可能会有更多的选票通过邮寄或在9月和10月投票。这有可能导致报道失灵：2018年，有线新闻评论员整个选举之夜都在暗示“蓝色浪潮”尚未到来。但他们只是不耐烦了：几周后加州最后一场竞选被召集时，民主党的激增就出现了。如果2016年大选是在预计由于冠状病毒危机而导致的邮寄投票激增的情况下进行的，宾夕法尼亚州的结果可能要到感恩节才会被计算在内。
然后是持续的特朗普时代的政治危机，通常是在Twitter和Facebook上推动的。上周四，特朗普总统再次试图用虚假的欺诈指控质疑邮寄投票。如果你想看看11月会发生什么，那就看看2018年吧，当时特朗普在Twitter上发帖称，“呼吁举行新的选举？”当亚利桑那州的共和党参议员提名人在邮寄选票清点时落后了。
这些都是艰巨的挑战。媒体专门打上一场战争，这个周期避免了2016年的错误，做得不错。记者们指责特朗普的谎言，表示对民调持怀疑态度，并避免将政治变成一项运动。
但美国媒体在美国选举中扮演着奇怪的超大角色，占据了大多数国家的国家选举委员会的位置。
在这里，媒体实际上汇集了50个州的结果，将它们制成表格，然后宣布胜利者。而且-我们真的忍不住-媒体建立了叙事来解释发生了什么。这项任务最令人难忘的是在2000年，当时有关乔治·W·布什(George W.Bush)赢得佛罗里达州的不准确说法导致副总统阿尔·戈尔(Al Gore)疯狂地收回了当晚早些时候向布什提出的让步，随后数周的不确定性也随之而来。
华丽的画面和冷静自信的主持人体现了电视花絮的悠久传统。当CBS在1952年发明了选举之夜戏剧性的投票预测和官方电话的传统时，它给它的布景配备了一台闪烁的雷明顿兰德大学(Remington Rand Univac)电脑。闪烁的装置是一场很好的表演。但正如历史学家吉尔·莱波勒在她的播客“最后的档案”中指出的那样，这台电脑是一个道具，一个赝品。
电视节目总是华而不实，但逐县选举制度的基础是巴洛克式的和过时的。大流行意味着今年将有更多的人通过邮寄投票，在没有处理这些选票经验的州。
达特茅斯大学的政治学家布伦丹·尼汉(Brendan Nyhan)说，“人们对这些令人惊叹的图表做了很多规划，但没有足够的规划来避免破坏人们对美国选举制度的信任。”尼汉是今年4月一份关于如何在疫情爆发期间进行公平选举的报告的作者之一。“这不会是很棒的电视节目，可能不会是病毒式的内容，但这是事实。”
一些特别靠谱的记者正试图奠定基础。美国全国广播公司(NBC)的查克·托德(Chuck Todd)在6月份表示，他一直在做关于选举的“重大噩梦”，他第一次阅读的时事通讯一直在提到“选举周”，而不是选举日。
但在大多数新闻机构和大型社交媒体平台的最高层，高管和内部人士告诉我，今年的情况完全没有不同-以及如何让观众为之做好准备。
尽管我采访的主持人和新闻高管都很认真地对待准备工作，但许多人似乎都在为这次选举做准备，就像他们过去为其他选举做的那样，一些人对我的危言耸听不屑一顾。
NBC新闻的总裁诺亚·奥本海姆(Noah Oppenheim)说：“我们不想制造一个混乱和混乱的自我实现的预言，也不想以某种方式暗示这是一个注定的结果，”NBC新闻的总裁诺亚·奥本海姆(Noah Oppenheim)说。
奥本海姆的乐观态度有点难以解释。4月份关于举行公平选举的报告向媒体提出了两项建议，但大多被忽略了。首先，开展一场激烈的竞选活动，向选民解释这一过程今年将如何实际运作。第二，教育公众耐心。
这不是媒体的本能。今年2月，美国有线电视新闻网(Cnn)采取了相反的做法，当时爱荷华州党团会议报告结果的速度很慢，网络上挂上了一个“倒计时”时钟，不耐烦地用脚敲打着结果，并发出信号，表明缓慢而仔细的计票有问题。
许多政治人士建议的另一项规模较小但意义重大的改变是：取消“选区报告百分比”这一误导性的措施。在宾夕法尼亚州和密歇根州等州，100%的选区报告选举日的结果是很容易的-但邮寄的选票堆积在仓库里，没有统计。
有一些令人鼓舞的迹象。美国有线电视新闻网(CNN)和美联社(The Associated Press)等媒体投入了比往常多得多的报道资源，告诉观众选举是如何进行的，并降低了他们对快速结果的期望。
美联社副执行主编大卫·斯科特(David Scott)说：“期望在选举之夜有那种电影般的体验总是不公平的标准。”
CNN华盛顿分社社长山姆·费斯特和哥伦比亚广播公司新闻选举和调查主任安东尼·萨尔万托都告诉我，他们已经不再使用选区报告百分比这一指标。
“纽约时报”高级编辑史蒂夫·杜内斯(Steve Duenes)说，“纽约时报”正在考虑替代单一的预测性针头，这种针头在2016年给读者带来了错误的信心，并正在寻找“一系列工具”。
但最重要的是，这一刻需要的是耐心。祝你好运。
与我交谈过的人中，没有人真正知道有线电视演讲者或Twitter上的讨价还价者会如何填补数小时、数天，甚至可能是数周的时间，也不知道如何冷静、谨慎地看待那些疯狂的指控-舞弊的投票机，出现在投票站的神秘外来者大巴-这些指控在每个选举之夜浮出水面，结果都会在白天化为乌有。
Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)在周六的一次简短采访中告诉我，他计划让观众为选举后的时期做好准备。他说，该网站计划进行一轮教育，目的是“让人们准备好接受这样一个事实，即很可能需要几天或几周的时间才能清点这些东西--这没有什么不对或非法的。”他说，Facebook正在考虑对过早宣称胜利或其他有关结果的声明制定新的规则。他补充说，该公司的选举中心将依靠通讯社提供最终结果。
人们很容易说，负责任的声音应该闭上嘴，换几天到熔岩地板，给善良的当地志愿者时间来计票。然而，这只会把对话让给最不负责、最阴谋论的声音。
俄亥俄州共和党国务卿弗兰克·拉罗斯(Frank LaRose)表示，他希望等待结果的时间能成为一种公民课程，播放志愿者将选票送入机器的镜头。加州民主党国务卿亚历克斯·帕迪利亚(Alex Padilla)建议电视公司借鉴好莱坞的模式：“你不能把”选举日“想象成一部电影--你必须把它当作三部曲，”他说。
但方便的是，一群名为过渡廉洁项目(Transition Integrity Project)的前政府高级官员实际上玩弄了四种可能的情况，其中一种看起来与2016年没有太大不同：乔·拜登(Joe Biden)在民众中大获全胜，选举中以微弱劣势落败，这可能是在宾夕法尼亚州计票数周后达成的。在他们的军事演习中，他们让希拉里·克林顿(Hillary Clinton)的竞选主席约翰·波德斯塔(John Podesta)扮演拜登先生。他们预计他会在投票结果出来时认输，就像克林顿夫人一样。
但扮演拜登的波德斯塔说，他觉得自己的政党不会让他让步，这让组织者感到震惊。他声称选民受到压制，说服威斯康星州和密歇根州州长将支持拜登的选举人派往选举人团。
在这种情况下，加利福尼亚州、俄勒冈州和华盛顿州随后威胁称，如果特朗普按计划上任，他们将脱离美国。众议院任命拜登为总统；参议院和白宫坚持支持特朗普。在这种情况下，这个国家不再从媒体那里寻找线索，而是等待军方会采取什么行动。
note_en:
Picture this Thanksgiving: turkey, football (maybe), tenser-than-usual interactions with relatives. And perhaps a new tradition: finding out who actually won the presidential election.
 The coronavirus crisis means that states like Pennsylvania may be counting mail-in ballots for weeks, while President Trump tweets false allegations about fraud. And the last barriers between American democracy and a deep political crisis may be television news and some version of that maddening needle on The New York Times website.
 I spoke last week to executives, TV hosts and election analysts across leading American newsrooms, and I was struck by the blithe confidence among some top managers and hosts, who generally said they’ve handled complicated elections before and can do so again. And I was alarmed by the near panic among some of the people paying the closest attention — the analysts and producers trying, and often failing, to get answers from state election officials about how and when they will count the ballots and report results.
 “The nerds are freaking out,” said Brandon Finnigan, the founder of Decision Desk HQ, which delivers election results to media outlets. “I don’t think it’s penetrated enough in the average viewer’s mind that there’s not going to be an election night. The usual razzmatazz of a panel sitting around discussing election results — that’s dead,” he said.
 First, there’s already a shift underway from a single-day, in-person election. In the 2018 midterms,  only 60 percent of the votes were cast in person on Election Day. More votes will probably be sent in this year by mail or cast in September and October. That risks coverage misfires: In 2018, cable news commentators spent  election night suggesting that the “blue wave” hadn’t arrived. But they were simply impatient: The Democratic surge showed up when the final California races were called weeks later. If the 2016 election had been conducted amid the expected surge in mail-in voting because of the coronavirus crisis, the Pennsylvania results might not have been counted until Thanksgiving.
 Then, there’s the continuing Trump-era political crisis, often driven on Twitter and Facebook. President Trump last Thursday again  sought to call mail-in voting into question with false claims about fraud. If you want a glimpse of how this could play out in November, look to 2018, when Mr. Trump  tweeted the suggestion, “Call for a new election?” when the Republican nominee for Senate in Arizona fell behind as mail ballots were counted.
 These are hard challenges. The media specializes in fighting the last war, and has done a decent job this cycle of avoiding the mistakes of 2016. Reporters are calling out Mr. Trump’s falsehoods, showing skepticism about polls and avoiding turning politics into a sport.
 But the American media plays a bizarrely outsize role in American elections, occupying the place of most countries’ national election commissions.
 Here, the media actually assembles the results from 50 states, tabulates them and declares a victor. And — we can’t really help ourselves — the media establishes the narrative to explain what happened. That task was most memorably mishandled in 2000, when inaccurate calls that George W. Bush had won Florida led to a wild retraction by Vice President Al Gore of the concession he had offered to Mr. Bush earlier that evening, followed by weeks of uncertainty.
 The flashy graphics and sober, confident hosts embody a long tradition of television flimflam. When CBS invented the election night tradition of dramatic vote projections and official calls in 1952, it outfitted its set with a blinking, Remington Rand Univac computer. The blinking device made for a good show. But the computer was a prop, a fake, as the historian Jill Lepore noted in her podcast,  The Last Archive.
 The TV presentation is always slick, but the underpinnings of county-by-county electoral systems are baroque and antiquated. And the pandemic means more people will vote by mail this year, in states with little experience processing those votes.
 “There’s a lot of planning for the whiz-bang graphics, and not enough planning for avoiding undermining trust in the American electoral system,” said Brendan Nyhan, a Dartmouth political scientist and one of the authors of an April  report on how to run a fair election during the pandemic. “It’s not going to be great TV, it might not be viral content, but it’s the truth.”
 Some particularly wonky journalists are trying to lay the groundwork. NBC’s Chuck Todd said in June that he has been having “major nightmares” about the election, and his First Read newsletter has been  referring to “election week” instead of Election Day.
 But at the highest levels of most news organizations and the big social media platforms, executives and insiders told me that it simply hasn’t sunk in how different this year is going to be — and how to prepare audiences for it.
 Though the hosts and news executives I talked to all take preparations seriously, many seemed to be preparing for this election as they have for others in the past, and some waved off my alarmism.
 “We don’t want to create a self-fulfilling prophecy of chaos and confusion or suggest somehow that that’s a preordained outcome,” said the president of NBC News, Noah Oppenheim.
 Mr. Oppenheim’s optimism is a bit hard to justify. The April report on running a fair election offers two recommendations for the media, which it’s mostly been ignoring. First, undertake an intense campaign to explain to voters how the process will actually work this year. And second, teach the public patience.
 That’s not the media’s instinct. CNN did the opposite this February, when the Iowa caucuses were slow to report results and the network put on a “count-up” clock, impatiently tapping its foot for a result and signaling that there’s something wrong with a slow, careful count.
 Another, smaller but important change that many political types suggest: Get rid of the misleading “percent of precincts reporting” measure. In states like Pennsylvania and Michigan, it would be easy to have 100 percent of precincts reporting their Election Day results — but have mail-in votes piled up in a warehouse, uncounted.
 There are some encouraging signs. CNN and The Associated Press, among others, have devoted far more reporting resources than usual to informing audiences just how elections work and to lowering their expectations of quick results.
 “It’s always an unfair standard to expect that kind of movie-like experience on election night,” said David Scott, deputy managing editor at the AP.
 And CNN’s Washington bureau chief, Sam Feist, and the CBS News elections and surveys director, Anthony Salvanto, both told me they’ve moved away from using the percent of precincts reporting measure.
 A top Times editor, Steve Duenes, said The Times was considering alternatives to the single, predictive needle that offered readers false confidence in 2016, and is looking at a “range of tools.”
 But what the moment calls for, most of all, is patience. And good luck with that.
 Nobody I talked to had any real idea how cable talkers or Twitter take-mongers would fill hours, days and, possibly, weeks of counting or how to apply a sober, careful lens to the wild allegations — rigged voting machines, mysterious buses of outsiders turning up at poll sites — that surface every election night, only to dissolve in the light of day.
 Facebook’s chief executive, Mark Zuckerberg, told me in a brief interview on Saturday that he’s planning to brace his audience for the postelection period. He said the site planned a round of education aimed at “getting people ready for the fact that there’s a high likelihood that it takes days or weeks to count this — and there’s nothing wrong or illegitimate about that.” And he said that Facebook is considering new rules regarding premature claims of victory or other statements about the results. He added that the company’s election center will rely on wire services for definitive results.
 It’s tempting to say responsible voices should keep their mouths shut and switch over for a few days to  Floor Is Lava, and give the nice local volunteers time to count the votes. That, however, would just cede the conversation to the least responsible, and conspiratorial, voices.
 The Republican secretary of state of Ohio, Frank LaRose, said he hoped that the time spent waiting for results could become a kind of civics lesson, with footage of volunteers feeding ballots into machines. Alex Padilla, the Democratic California secretary of state, suggested that television companies look to a Hollywood model: “You can’t think of Election Day as a single movie — you have to treat it as maybe a trilogy,” he said.
  But conveniently, a group of former top government officials called the Transition Integrity Project actually  gamed four possible scenarios, including one that doesn’t look that different from 2016: a big popular win for Joe Biden, and a narrow electoral defeat, presumably reached after weeks of counting the votes in Pennsylvania. For their war game, they cast John Podesta, who was Hillary Clinton’s campaign chairman, in the role of Mr. Biden. They expected him, when the votes came in, to concede, just as Mrs. Clinton had.
 But Mr. Podesta, playing Mr. Biden, shocked the organizers by saying he felt his party wouldn’t let him concede. Alleging voter suppression, he persuaded the governors of Wisconsin and Michigan to send pro-Biden electors to the Electoral College.
 In that scenario, California, Oregon, and Washington then threatened to secede from the United States if Mr. Trump took office as planned. The House named Mr. Biden president; the Senate and White House stuck with Mr. Trump. At that point in the scenario, the nation stopped looking to the media for cues, and waited to see what the military would do.
title: 马克·扎克伯格表示，Facebook正在考虑围绕选举结果声明制定新规则，包括过早宣布获胜
title_en: Mark Zuckerberg says Facebook is mulling new rules around statements regarding election results including premature claims of victory

note:
这是我的选择，但我开始希望不是这样，我觉得自己没有资格。我当了20年的警长，所以我想我应该习惯于做决定，但我总是在脑子里迷失方向。我会在办公室里看着一块空白的电脑屏幕，然后突然意识到已经过去了整整一个小时。我很担心。我对一切都很担心。我想出来的每一种可能性都是不好的。
州长告诉我们，我们必须在8月17日向学生开放我们的学校，否则我们会错过百分之五的资金。我在亚利桑那州偏远的地方经营着一个高需求区。我们90%是西班牙裔，超过90%的午餐是免费和折扣的。这些孩子需要我们能弄到的每一块钱。但是Covid在这个地区蔓延，击中了我的员工，现在感觉就像有人用枪指着我的头。我已经因为这种病毒失去了一位老师。即使这会让我们付出更多的生命，我也要冒着重新敞开大门的风险吗？或者我们远程办学，结果剥夺了这些孩子？
这就是你的经典单马镇。想象一下约翰·韦恩骑马穿过仙人掌之类的场景。我是学校的负责人，高中校长，有时在课间还担任篮球裁判员。这是骨干员工，我们的平均年薪约为4万美元。我没什么可剪的了。我们正在购买虚拟学习的新程序，并试图为我们所有的孩子购买热点和iPad。我们预算的5%是数十万美元。这从何而来呢？我可能会失去教学职位或基本课程，除非我们以某种方式开始运作。
我每天都在大楼里，给门消毒，并测量教室的空间。我们还没有收到有机玻璃护栏的订单，所以我们正在裁剪浴帘，试着凑合着用。这是一个又一个障碍。就在上周，我发现我们有另一名检测呈阳性的工作人员，所以我查看了OSHA和疾病预防控制中心的指导意见，并试图弄清楚方案。我不是这方面的专家，但我在联系人追踪方面尽了最大努力。我给10个员工打了电话，告诉他们可能接触到了病毒。我安排了不同的车，把我们都送到了考点。我的一些员工在哭。他们已经看到了可能发生的事情，他们带着我不能总是回答的问题来找我。“我们全家都需要检查吗？”“我需要隔离多久？”“如果这个病毒像对伯德夫人那样袭击我怎么办？”
我们已经拿到两个测试了-都是阳性的。我们还在等八个人。这使得我的员工中有11%的人变得令人羡慕，自3月份以来，我们的大楼里就没有一个学生。我们的部分设施已经关闭进行净化，但是除非我想穿上防护服走进去，否则我们就没有人去净化它了。我们已经看到这种病毒对我们的维修部、运输、餐饮服务和教职员工造成的影响。就像这个地区要逐案关闭一样。我不明白怎么会有人期望我们这个月以一种感觉安全的方式重新开放大楼。就像他们在告诉我们：“好吧。夏天结束了。已经很久了。是时候恢复正常了。“。但从什么时候起这种病毒开始按我们的时间表运作了？
我梦想回归正常。我很乐意敞开心扉。这些孩子现在很受伤。我不需要政客来告诉我这些。我们这个学区只有300名学生，他们就像家人一样。我妻子是这里的一名教师，我们有四个孩子在这些学校上学。我知道谁的父母从铜矿下岗，谁吃不饱饭。今年夏天我们送早餐和午餐，每天送的饭菜比学生还多。我接到处理贫困、抑郁、孤独、无聊等问题的家庭打来的电话。这些孩子中的一些人现在正在荒野中，学校是他们最好的地方。我们都同意这一点。但每次我在8月17日开始播放这一幕时，我都会反胃。我们超过四分之一的学生和祖父母住在一起。这些孩子很容易感染这种病毒，传播并带回家。这不安全。它不可能是安全的。
如果你有其他想法，我很抱歉，但那是幻想。孩子们会生病，甚至更糟。家人会死的。老师会死的。
伯德太太做的每件事都是对的。她遵循了所有的协议。如果在大流行期间教室里有一个安全、可控的环境，那就是它了。我们有三个老师共用一个房间，这样他们就可以教一个虚拟的暑期学校。他们太小心了。这要追溯到6月份，当时这里的病例开始激增。孩子们都在家里，但老师们想在教室里聚在一起，这样他们就可以在新技术上合作了。我觉得这是个好主意。房间很大。他们可以互相观看，互相学习。伯德太太是个桅杆
起初她以为是鼻窦感染。医生是这么告诉她的，但情况越来越糟。我接到电话说她被紧急送往医院了。她的氧气量很低，他们马上就给她戴上了呼吸机。另外两名老师在同一个周末开始感觉不舒服，所以他们去做了检查。在接下来的一个月里，他们俩都过得很糟糕。伯德太太的丈夫得了病，住院了。她哥哥得了病就去世了。伯德太太抗争了几个星期，直到她再也打不动了。
我已经在脑子里想了一千遍了。我们遗漏了哪些预防措施？我还能做些什么呢？我没有答案。这是三个负责任的成年人，在一个原本空荡荡的教室里，他们努力相互保护。我们还是控制不了它。这就是让我害怕的。
我们把全体员工召集到一起进行悲伤咨询。我们虚拟地做了，超过了Zoom。这里有悲伤，也有太多的恐惧。我的妻子是我们小学的一名老师，她患有哮喘。她向我解释说，每个看到她的孩子都会自动给她一个拥抱。他们在早晨到达-拥抱。离开去休息-拥抱。午餐拥抱。更衣柜的拥抱。那是一整天了。即使我们把每件事都做得尽善尽美，细菌也会在学校内传播。我们共用一个空间。我们的空气是一样的。
我们的一群老师告诉我，如果我们这个月开学，他们就会申请退休。他们在说：“请不要让我们回去。这太疯狂了。我们正在将整个社区置于危险之中。“。
他们是对的。我百分之百同意他们的看法。老师们没有安全感。大多数家长在一项调查中表示，他们“非常担心”把孩子送回学校。那我们为什么要被胁迫开张呢？这个区还没准备好开放。我不能再让更多的人生病了。为什么他们要威胁我们的资金？我一直在等待更高级别的人从我手中夺走这个决定，让他们醒悟过来。我在等待真正的领导，但也许这不会发生。
是我呀。这是我职业生涯中最重大的决定，有一点我可以肯定的是，无论是哪种情况，这都会带来伤害。
note_en:
This is my choice, but I’m starting to wish that it wasn’t. I don’t feel qualified. I’ve been a superintendent for 20 years, so I guess I should be used to making decisions, but I keep getting lost in my head. I’ll be in my office looking at a blank computer screen, and then all of the sudden I realize a whole hour’s gone by. I’m worried. I’m worried about everything. Each possibility I come up with is a bad one.
    The governor has told us we have to open our schools to students on August 17th, or else we miss out on five percent of our funding. I run a high-needs district in middle-of-nowhere Arizona. We’re 90 percent Hispanic and more than 90 percent free-and-reduced lunch. These kids need every dollar we can get. But covid is spreading all over this area and hitting my staff, and now it feels like there’s a gun to my head. I already lost one teacher to this virus. Do I risk opening back up even if it’s going to cost us more lives? Or do we run school remotely and end up depriving these kids?
  This is your classic one-horse town. Picture John Wayne riding through cactuses and all that. I’m superintendent, high school principal and sometimes the basketball referee during recess. This is a skeleton staff, and we pay an average salary of about 40,000 a year. I’ve got nothing to cut. We’re buying new programs for virtual learning and trying to get hotspots and iPads for all our kids. Five percent of our budget is hundreds of thousands of dollars. Where’s that going to come from? I might lose teaching positions or basic curriculum unless we somehow get up and running.
    I’ve been in the building every day, sanitizing doors and measuring out space in classrooms. We still haven’t received our order of Plexiglas barriers, so we’re cutting up shower curtains and trying to make do with that. It’s one obstacle after the next. Just last week I found out we had another staff member who tested positive, so I went through the guidance from OSHA and the CDC and tried to figure out the protocols. I’m not an expert at any of this, but I did my best with the contact tracing. I called 10 people on staff and told them they’d had a possible exposure. I arranged separate cars and got us all to the testing site. Some of my staff members were crying. They’ve seen what can happen, and they’re coming to me with questions I can’t always answer. “Does my whole family need to get tested?” “How long do I have to quarantine?” “What if this virus hits me like it did Mrs. Byrd?”
  We got back two of those tests already — both positive. We’re still waiting on eight more. That makes 11 percent of my staff that’s gotten covid, and we haven’t had a single student in our buildings since March. Part of our facility is closed down for decontamination, but we don’t have anyone left to decontaminate it unless I want to put on my hazmat suit and go in there. We’ve seen the impacts of this virus on our maintenance department, on transportation, on food service, on faculty. It’s like this district is shutting down case by case. I don’t understand how anyone could expect us to reopen the building this month in a way that feels safe. It’s like they’re telling us: “Okay. Summer’s over. It’s been long enough. Time to get back to normal.” But since when has this virus operated on our schedule?
  I dream about going back to normal. I’d love to be open. These kids are hurting right now. I don’t need a politician to tell me that. We only have 300 students in this district, and they’re like family. My wife is a teacher here, and we had four kids go through these schools. I know whose parents are laid off from the copper mine and who doesn’t have enough to eat. We delivered breakfast and lunches this summer, and we gave out more meals each day than we have students. I get phone calls from families dealing with poverty issues, depression, loneliness, boredom. Some of these kids are out in the wilderness right now, and school is the best place for them. We all agree on that. But every time I start to play out what that looks like on August 17th, I get sick to my stomach. More than a quarter of our students live with grandparents. These kids could very easily catch this virus, spread it and bring it back home. It’s not safe. There’s no way it can be safe.
  If you think anything else, I’m sorry, but it’s a fantasy. Kids will get sick, or worse. Family members will die. Teachers will die.
    Mrs. Byrd did everything right. She followed all the protocols. If there’s such a thing as a safe, controlled environment inside a classroom during a pandemic, that was it. We had three teachers sharing a room so they could teach a virtual summer school. They were so careful. This was back in June, when cases here were starting to spike. The kids were at home, but the teachers wanted to be together in the classroom so they could team up on the new technology. I thought that was a good idea. It’s a big room. They could watch and learn from each other. Mrs. Byrd was a master teacher. She’d been here since 1982, and she was always coming up with creative ideas. They delivered care packages to the elementary students so they could sprout beans for something hands-on at home, and then the teachers all took turns in front of the camera. All three of them wore masks. They checked their temperatures. They taught on their own devices and didn’t share anything, not even a pencil.
  At first she thought it was a sinus infection. That’s what the doctor told her, but it kept getting worse. I got a call that she’d been rushed to the hospital. Her oxygen was low, and they put her on a ventilator pretty much right away. The other two teachers started feeling sick the same weekend, so they went to get tested. They both had it bad for the next month. Mrs. Byrd’s husband got it and was hospitalized. Her brother got it and passed away. Mrs. Byrd fought for a few weeks until she couldn’t anymore.
  I’ve gone over it in my head a thousand times. What precautions did we miss? What more could I have done? I don’t have an answer. These were three responsible adults in an otherwise empty classroom, and they worked hard to protect each other. We still couldn’t control it. That’s what scares me.
    We got the whole staff together for grief counseling. We did it virtually, over Zoom. There’s sadness, and it’s also so much fear. My wife is one of our teachers in the primary grade, and she has asthma. She was explaining to me how every kid who sees her automatically gives her a hug. They arrive in the morning — hug. Leave for recess — hug. Lunch — hug. Locker — hug. That’s all day. Even if we do everything perfectly, germs are going to spread inside a school. We share the same space. We share the same air.
  A bunch of our teachers have told me they will put in for retirement if we open up this month. They’re saying: “Please don’t make us go back. This is crazy. We’re putting the whole community at risk.”
  They’re right. I agree with them 100 percent. Teachers don’t feel safe. Most parents said in a survey that they’re “very concerned” about sending their kids back to school. So why are we getting bullied into opening? This district isn’t ready to open. I can’t have more people getting sick. Why are they threatening our funding? I keep waiting for someone higher up to take this decision out of my hands and come to their senses. I’m waiting for real leadership, but maybe it’s not going to happen.
  It’s me. It’s the biggest decision of my career, and the one part I’m certain about is it’s going to hurt either way.
title: 亚利桑那州的一名督学关于安全重新开学：“这是一种幻想。”
title_en: An Arizona superintendent on safely reopening schools: 'It's a fantasy'

note:
特斯拉最近因超过丰田成为全球最有价值的汽车公司而登上新闻头条，但如果你比较两家公司去年的汽车销量，就会发现这一事实相当令人难以置信：
当然，特斯拉实现其价值更多的是基于未来的潜力，而不是现在的成就。在两极分化的首席执行官埃隆·马斯克(Elon Musk)的领导下，该公司已经为生产电动汽车铺平了道路，而此时全世界正朝着使用更可持续的能源的方向发展。
但本周早些时候，马斯克在Twitter上发表了微妙的评论，可能会在很大程度上颠覆汽车业。在Teslarati上发表了一篇文章，强调德国汽车制造商试图弥合特斯拉技术与自己技术之间的差距，马斯克在推特上写道：
马斯克在推特上写道，特斯拉对授权软件和提供动力总成和电池持开放态度。我们只是在努力加速可持续能源，而不是粉碎竞争对手！
除了在电动汽车生产(以及支持这些产品所需的更大样式的电池)方面领先于竞争对手之外，特斯拉在将现代技术应用于其车辆方面也走在了前列。事实上，许多人将特斯拉描述为一家碰巧制造汽车的科技公司。
相比之下，马斯克曾多次谈到实际生产质量稳定的汽车以及交付汽车所面临的挑战。他一度将特斯拉的旅程描述为从生产地狱到交付物流地狱。
在光谱的另一端，有传统的汽车制造商，如丰田、大众、通用和福特。很大程度上是因为特斯拉的崛起，这些公司被迫推进自己的电动汽车努力。这是以巨大的成本和资源使用为代价的，因为他们基本上是从头开始的。
然而，传统的汽车制造商在特斯拉的弱点方面表现出色：即制造和交付。自从他们制造汽车这么长时间以来，他们已经发展了巨大的工厂，以及一致和精致的工艺。
但是，如果特斯拉能够与汽车制造商达成协议，授权其优势--软件和电池技术--会怎么样呢？
传统汽车制造商可以(几乎)立即赶上特斯拉的技术，使他们能够专注于自己擅长的领域：制造和交付。
特斯拉从许可协议中赚了很多钱，使其继续在软件创新领域处于领先地位。
普通民众受益最大，因为整个汽车业更接近使用更可持续能源的目标，并在其车辆中融入新技术。
如果你对马斯克的推文感到惊讶，你不应该感到惊讶。事实上，多年来，马斯克一直坚称，他的首要目标不是与规模更大的汽车制造商竞争，而是争取他们的支持。
早在2014年，马斯克就在一篇著名的博客文章中阐述了这一点。在那篇博文中，他宣布特斯拉不会对其他想要使用其技术的公司提起专利诉讼。马斯克说，这是本着开源运动的精神，为了推动电动汽车技术的发展而做的。
马斯克解释说，特斯拉最初寻求专利，是为了防止更大的汽车公司想要复制其技术，然后利用他们的大量资源让特斯拉倒闭。但没过多久，它就意识到了相反的事实：在发表这篇文章的时候，传统的汽车制造商的电动汽车项目规模很小，甚至根本不存在。
马斯克写道，鉴于每年新车年产量接近1亿辆，全球车队约为20亿辆，特斯拉不可能以足够快的速度制造电动汽车来解决碳危机。我们真正的竞争对手不是生产非特斯拉电动车的涓涓细流，而是每天从世界各地的工厂涌出的大量汽油车。
所以，我们来了。六年后，特斯拉不再是这个街区的新成员。不再是其他人不屑一顾的后起之秀汽车公司。
但这不一定是一场零和游戏。它甚至不一定非得是一场竞争，至少不是与特斯拉的竞争。
如果老牌汽车制造商够聪明，他们会抓住机会谈判一项对自己和特斯拉都有意义的授权协议。
因为谁先弄清楚，谁就可能把自己打造成世界上第二大最有价值的汽车公司。
title: 埃隆·马斯克悄悄透露了一项可能改变汽车业的计划
title_en: Elon Musk Quietly Revealed a Plan That Could Change the Auto Industry

note:
周日下午，一艘略微烧焦的宇宙飞船从蓝天坠落，坠入平静的墨西哥湾。NASA宇航员道格·赫尔利(Doug Hurley)和鲍勃·本肯(Bob Behnken)从轨道安全返回，为近64天的任务画上了句号，这项任务证明了SpaceX，Crew Dragon建造的一艘新航天器的可行性。这是一次圆满的成功。
SpaceX公司总裁兼首席运营官格温·肖特威尔(Gwynne Shotwell)在飞行后的新闻发布会上说，这是不寻常的一天。在几个月的焦虑之后，这是一个巨大的解脱。
NASA和SpaceX现在将密切研究在这次试飞中收集的数据，基于这些分析，“乘员龙”运载火箭很可能获得国际空间站作战任务的认证。这些任务中的第一次可能最快在9月下旬到来，届时美国国家航空航天局(NASA)的三名宇航员和日本宇航员将开始为期六个月的在轨工作。
今年夏天，“龙”的试飞结束了NASA在载人航天能力上长达九年的差距。自2011年7月以来，航天局一直依靠俄罗斯的联盟号飞船将宇航员送到空间站。现在，美国国家航空航天局有了自己的交通工具。在接下来的一年内，美国的第二架飞行器--波音公司的“星际客机”也将与三名宇航员一起进行首次试飞。希望以后再也不会有这样的差距。
美国宇航局局长吉姆·布里登斯廷(Jim Bridenstine)说，我们正在进入一个太空飞行的新时代，他一直支持公私合作，以利用NASA的技术诀窍和私营行业的驱动力来推动航天事业向前发展。商业船员计划确实证明了我们如何前进的商业模式，以及我们如何做比以往任何时候都更多的事情。
周六晚上，“龙”从空间站脱离，然后花了大约19个小时进行了一系列a阶段燃烧，以便排队等待重返大气层降落在墨西哥湾。NASA和SpaceX在佛罗里达州周围有七个地点可供选择，由于热带风暴伊萨亚斯沿着该州大西洋一侧的路径，他们选择了墨西哥湾一侧。
墨西哥湾的天气再平静不过了，风几乎静止，海面风平浪静。被赫尔利和本肯命名为奋进号的机组人员龙飞船，在返回大气层的非常高的温度下继续进行，在此期间，等离子体阻止了大约6分钟的通信。但在这次停电后，航天器毫发无损地进入了较低的大气层，打开了减速伞，紧随其后的是电源，然后平稳地溅落下来。
机组操作负责工程师迈克·海曼(Mike Heiman)通过无线电向机组人员打趣地说，欢迎回到地球，感谢你驾驶SpaceX。
SpaceX和NASA在将航天器移到Go Navigator回收船上后，提前了他们的时间表。然而，就在舱门打开之前，技术人员注意到四氧化二氮的含量略高于正常水平，四氧化二氮是一种氧化剂，有助于为太空中的推进器提供动力。尽管机组人员本可以安全撤离，但公司和航天局花了时间来净化烟雾。
然后，宇航员们准备好在两个多月后呼吸地球上的空气。就在他离开飞船之前，赫尔利祝贺SpaceX完成任务，并感谢他们的奉献。他说，任何触摸过奋进号的人都应该花点时间珍惜这一天，特别是考虑到今年发生的所有事情。
回到SpaceX总部，他们正在珍惜这一天。在加利福尼亚州的任务控制室内，在机组人员安全离开飞船后，他们开了香槟。肖特维尔说：“我们应该把这当作一个跳板，与阿耳特弥斯·普罗甘姆一起做更难的事情，然后再去火星。”
note_en:
On Sunday afternoon a slightly charred spacecraft dropped out of the blue sky, and splashed down into a placid Gulf of Mexico. The safe return of NASA astronauts Doug Hurley and Bob Behnken from orbit capped a nearly 64-day mission that proved the viability of a new spacecraft built by SpaceX, Crew Dragon. It was a complete success.
 "This was an extraordinary day," said Gwynne Shotwell, president and chief operating officer of SpaceX, during a post-flight news conference. "It was an enormous relief after months of anxiety."
 NASA and SpaceX will now closely study data collected during this test flight, and based upon this analysis the Crew Dragon vehicle will likely be certified for operational missions to the International Space Station. The first of those missions may come as soon as late September, when three NASA astronauts and on Japanese astronaut are due to begin a six-month stint in orbit.
  This summer's test flight of Dragon brings to a close a nine-year gap in human spaceflight capability at NASA. Since July, 2011, the space agency has relied on Russian Soyuz spacecraft to get its astronauts to the space station. Now, NASA has its own transportation. Within the next year a second U.S. vehicle, Boeing's Starliner, should also make its first test flight with three astronauts. Hopefully, there will never be such a gap again.
 "We are entering a new era of spaceflight," said NASA Administrator Jim Bridenstine, who has supported public-private partnerships to leverage NASA know-how and the drive of private industry to push spaceflight forward. "The Commercial Crew Program has really just proven the business model for how we go forward, and how we do more than we’ve ever been able to do before."
  Dragon undocked from the space station on Saturday night and then spent about 19 hours performing a series a phasing burns to line up for a reentry to land in the Gulf of Mexico. NASA and SpaceX had the choice of seven locations around the state of Florida, and chose the Gulf side due to the track of Tropical Storm Isaias along the Atlantic side of the state.
 The weather in the Gulf could not have been more sedate, with nearly stationary winds and calm seas. The Crew Dragon spacecraft, named  Endeavour by Hurley and Behnken, carried on through the very high temperatures of atmospheric return, during which plasma prevented communication for about six minutes. But after this blackout the spacecraft emerged into the lower atmosphere unscathed, popped its drogue parachutes, followed by its mains, and then a smooth splashdown.
 "Welcome back to planet Earth and thanks for flying SpaceX," quipped Mike Heiman, Crew Operations Responsible Engineer, over the radio to the crew.
 SpaceX and NASA were ahead of their timeline after moving the spacecraft onto the  GO Navigator recovery ship. However, just before hatch opening, technicians noted slightly above normal levels of dinitrogen tetroxide, an oxidizer that helps power thrusters in space. Although the crew could have egressed safely, the company and space agency took the time to purge the fumes.
  And then, the astronauts were ready to breathe air on planet Earth after more than two months. Just before he departed the spacecraft, Hurley congratulated SpaceX on the mission, and thanked them for their dedication. He said, "Anybody who has touched  Endeavour should take a moment to cherish this day, especially given all of the things that have happened this year."
 Back at SpaceX headquarters, they were cherishing this day. Inside the mission control room in California, they popped champagne after the crew safely emerged from the spacecraft. "We should think about this as a springboard to doing even harder things, with the Artemis Prorgam, and then moving on to Mars," Shotwell said.
title: 在一次精彩的飞行测试之后，美国国家航空航天局现在有了一次新的太空之旅。
title_en: After a splendid flight test, NASA now has a new ride to space

当我妹妹和我年轻的时候，我们喜欢放学回家，打开哥伦比亚广播公司(CBS)的肥皂剧“指引之光”(Guide Light)。我们只看了一小时节目的最后15分钟，但因为它不是特别微妙，所以即使是最复杂的情节也有足够的时间去追寻-比如瑞瓦·肖恩(Reva Shayne)，一个结过9次婚的角色，她作为脱口秀主持人拥有弧线，是一位灵媒，一个虚构岛屿的公主，也是一名前往内战和纳粹德国的时间旅行者，她不得不与多莉(Dolly)战斗，多莉是她最近的丈夫为了让她的孩子免受灰烬而为她制作的一个狡猾的克隆人。
“引路之光”始于1937年，当时是一档宣传名为“杜兹”(Duz)的肥皂剧的广播节目。(“杜兹包办一切。”)。当它在2009年停播时，它是广播史上播出时间最长的节目。直到最后，它的所有者不是CBS，而是宝洁公司(Procter&amp；Gamble)，它最初是一家肥皂公司，被认为是发明了美国现代广告的功臣。除了用电车和广告牌上的绘画来宣传自己的品牌外，该公司还开发了20多部广播剧和电视剧。第一部“奥昔多尔自己的马·珀金斯”(Oxydol‘s Own Ma Perkins)于1933年首播；最后一部“随着世界的转折”(As The World Turns)于2010年退出广播，到那时“肥皂剧”这个词已经独立出来。你甚至可以像我一样，在不知道他们与肥皂公司有任何关系的情况下观看他们。
在-19病毒爆发之前，人们很容易不经常或不太深入地考虑肥皂。在大流行早期，这种情况开始改变。我们了解了哪些流行歌曲的合唱可以让我们刷洗整整20秒；我们了解到，至少在封锁前，男厕所外面的队伍突然变长了--根据一项研究，可能是因为(根据一项研究)只有31%的男性以前有过上厕所后洗手的习惯。随着酿酒厂和酿酒厂转而生产洗手液，时报刊登了一篇文章，解释为什么老式肥皂实际上更能摧毁冠状病毒：肥皂分子的疏水尾巴与保护病毒的脂膜结合在一起，真正地将其撕裂，而它们的亲水性头部与将死亡病毒冲走的水结合在一起。像许多人一样，我对肥皂产生了一种新的欣赏，每次洗手时，我都满腹牢骚地想象着一个微观破坏的场景。
所以现在读一本医生写的书是很奇怪的，这本书对肥皂行业持批判态度，以一句话开始：“五年前，我不再洗澡了。”
让我立即澄清一下，詹姆斯·汉布林(James Hamblin)是“大西洋月刊”(The Atlantic)的特约撰稿人，也是“清洁：皮肤的新科学”(Riverhead)一书的作者，他仍然提倡定期洗手，这无疑是公共卫生领域一项改变世界的创新，在这个历史时刻尤其重要。(汉布林还写道，他“永远不会连续两天穿白大衣不洗”。)。但他对所有的擦洗和肥皂都持怀疑态度-更不用说保湿、除臭和血清和酸的应用了-我们身体最大的器官的其余部分都要接受这些擦洗和肥皂洗涤，以及那些花了很多钱来说服我们必须这样做才能保持清洁的公司。
肥皂是一项古老的发明，如此古老，以至于我们只能假设它是动物脂肪溢出到火灰中的幸运结果，一些人足够警觉地注意到了由此产生的泡沫的清洁能力。尽管如此，用碱液制成的早期版本可能会灼伤皮肤，而且更多的是用来洗衣服，而不是用来洗人。洗澡更常见的是用水、沙子、浮石、刮刀、油或香水--尽管在某些地方，整个概念被认为是危险的。一些历史记录表明，洗澡在西方世界相对罕见：马可波罗写道，他对印度和中国人洗澡的频率感到惊讶；艾哈迈德·本·法德兰(Ahmad Ibn Fadlan)在10世纪初从巴格达宫廷来到伏尔加河，他写道，他在旅途中遇到的人在吃饭、小便、小便或做爱后不洗澡，是“真主创造的最肮脏的生物”。法国历史学家朱尔斯·米歇尔(Jules Michelet)将欧洲中世纪描述为“一千年没有洗澡”。
在美国，用于皮肤的肥皂直到19世纪才开始普遍销售，很大程度上是作为一种利用肉类加工业的剩余物赚钱的方式，肉类加工业生产了大量未使用的动物脂肪。企业家们加入钾肥和制造肥皂，然后他们需要创造公众需求。这些早期的“肥皂剧”包括威廉·普罗克特(William Procter)和詹姆斯·甘布尔(James Gamble)，他们在与一对姐妹结婚后开始一起工作；另一对家族情侣，他们的公司名称最终从利弗兄弟(Lever Brothers)改为联合利华(Unilever)；还有一个名叫小威廉·瑞格利(William Wrigley，Jr.)的男子，他把口香糖作为舞会赠送出去。
另一项创新是创造，然后满足人们不知道自己拥有的需求。汉布林指出，“B.O.”一开始是一个营销术语，许多宣传为“抗菌”和“抗菌”的肥皂不如标准肥皂安全，留下了危险的化合物。(许多我们现在认为是肥皂的产品实际上是洗涤剂，由合成化合物制成。)。
汉布林写道，与此同时，肥皂公司为了扩大他们的产品线，“不得不兜售肥皂本身不够的想法-或者它的影响必须被更多的产品抵消，”汉布林写道。你的头发、身体、脸部，甚至一个家庭的不同成员都需要单独的肥皂。(据报道，阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦被问及为什么不使用新发明的剃须膏时，他回答说：“两个肥皂？这太复杂了！“)。为了抵消肥皂的干燥效应，你还需要其他产品-护发素、保湿剂、爽肤水。汉布林认为，1957年推出的多芬(Dove)的清洁力有所降低，因为它与保湿霜混合在一起，这是该行业开始转向销售一种根本起不到任何作用的产品的时刻。
汉布林停止洗澡的决定-他偶尔漱口-开始是一个实验，因为他开始质疑他认为是自己生活必需品的东西。他卖掉了他的车，取消了上网，并考虑住在面包车里，直到他的女朋友反对。但它也反映了对我们皮肤功能的新理解：作为一个活的、可渗透的“动态界面”，连接我们与周围的世界-“一个复杂、多样化的生态系统”，而不是一个应该是无菌和原始的屏障。他推断，肥皂不仅洗掉了天然油脂，还洗掉了许多让我们的皮肤成为它们家的微生物，破坏了它们本来可能实现的自然平衡。
汉布林开始了一系列相当杂乱无章、有时并不令人满意的冒险，目的是质疑干净意味着什么。他参观了布朗纳博士的魔力肥皂工厂，做了一个奇特的面部护理，并参加了一家更有光泽度的商店的开业仪式和各种产品博览会。一名推销员将男士专用肥皂从看起来像威士忌酒瓶的酒杯里倒进酒杯；另一名推销员试图向他推销一种“青春之泉干细胞保湿霜”，其中的干细胞来自南瓜。为了表明我们对化妆品成分的监管有多么宽松-欧盟禁止或限制个人护理产品中的1500种化学物质，而美国只有11种-他开了自己的护肤品公司。它被称为Brunson+Sterling(座右铭：“他妈的完美皮肤的男士护理”)，提供两盎司瓶装的随机配料组合，每瓶200美元。这个产品卖不出去，但它是合法的。
最有趣的是，汉布林遇到的人对护肤的意义有着截然不同的看法。一位患有痤疮的妇女尝试了各种方法，从擦洗到抗生素、Accutane和荷尔蒙节育。(痤疮部分是由痤疮皮肤杆菌引起的，痤疮是开抗生素处方的最常见原因之一。)。事情只会变得更糟，直到她放弃，不再做任何事情，发现自己的皮肤变白了。加拿大皮肤科医生桑迪·斯科特尼基(Sandy Skoticki)整个冬天都在乞求瘙痒的男性停止使用沐浴露，她想知道，过度洗涤和湿疹的增加是否不仅仅是相互关联的。(汉布林写道，因为斯科特尼基的病人想要开什么药，她“找到了一种让一些东西变得一无所有的方法”，通过倡导有计划的清洁-她的意思只是指停止使用清洁产品。)。一些科学家发现湿疹的症状--通常包括过多的金黄色葡萄球菌--可以用一种不同的细菌来治疗。
汉布林采访了免疫学和微生物学的专家，他们担心，对我们中的一些人来说，人类长期存在的卫生问题现在已经发生了逆转：不是太少，而是我们可能有太多的卫生问题。这些专家希望我们更广泛地考虑卫生问题--作为健康和平衡的问题，而不是无菌和纯净的问题。有了我们所有的肥皂、消毒剂和抗生素，再加上我们花了这么多时间呆在室内，远离尘土、动物和新鲜空气，我们的免疫系统产生了新的问题，我们的免疫系统错过了遇到良性触发因素的机会，相反，我们学会了对感知到的威胁反应过度。过度的卫生也可能是皮肤微生物群的一个问题，它的生态我们才刚刚开始了解。印第安纳州的过敏症专科医生马克·霍尔布雷奇(Mark Holbreich)发现，阿米什人的过敏、湿疹和其他皮肤问题的水平明显较低，即使与遗传和文化相关的群体相比也是如此，包括南达科他州的哈特利派(Hutterite)，他们的孩子通常在离农场更远的地方长大。朱莉·塞格雷(Julie Segre)发表了人类皮肤细菌和真菌多样性的第一张地图，她指出
就像医学上经常发生的那样，皮肤的重要性在它的工作方式出现问题时表现得最为明显。莱曼向我们讲述了20世纪初南卡罗来纳州普遍出现的一种叫青春痘的疼痛皮疹，它会导致“无法控制的腹泻”，并最终导致精神错乱，直到通过引入均衡饮食才最终治愈；这就是包装面包现在含有烟酸的原因。我们被介绍给患有着色性干皮病的孩子，这是一种遗传病，会破坏寻找紫外线对DNA损害的自然修复系统；他们有时被称为“午夜儿童”，因为他们需要躲避阳光，并以惊人的速度患皮肤癌。在另一种遗传性的大疱性表皮松解症中，没有蛋白质连接表皮和真皮，这意味着皮肤可以被“像扭转门把手一样轻微的剪切力”撕裂。患有这种疾病的年轻患者哈桑(Hassan)在接受开创性治疗时几乎没有皮肤了：医生采集了他的一些皮肤细胞，将它们暴露在一种携带健康版本突变基因的病毒中，然后在实验室里用它们长出了9平方英尺的新皮肤，他们成功地将这些皮肤移植到了哈桑的身体上。
汉布林专注于清洁，而莱曼则试图全面审视皮肤，快速浏览触觉、疼痛、纹身历史、黑色素科学、不同宗教如何看待裸体，以及我们的皮肤暴露在阳光下如何影响我们更广泛的健康。(你知道吗，狗和猫，可能是因为它们的皮毛阻碍了皮肤吸收阳光和产生维生素D的能力，所以它们会分泌一种油，当暴露在阳光下时，这种油会转化为维生素D。然后，它必须被口服，这是宠物总是舔自己的原因之一。)。莱曼指出，太阳对皮肤老化的影响甚至比时间本身更深远。他写道，他在一家诊所工作，错误地认为一个60多岁的女人是一个40多岁崇拜太阳的病人的女儿，而不是母亲。
虽然他带读者参观了一系列时髦的皮肤护理，从金·卡戴珊(Kim Kardashian)曾经喜欢的“吸血鬼面孔”到埃及艳后每天用驴奶洗澡，但他唯一完全认可的美容疗法是防晒、健康饮食，以及避免吸烟、过度饮酒和长期压力。莱曼写道：“在2008年的金融危机中，牛皮癣和湿疹的就诊达到了创纪录的高峰，这并不令人惊讶。”乳糜泻、克罗恩病、酒渣鼻和湿疹等疾病--所有这些疾病都涉及皮肤、免疫系统和肠道--揭示了这些系统是如何相互交织在一起的。例如，湿疹是婴儿是否会患上食物过敏的预测指标，人们发现，当一个人的饮食变得西方化时，痤疮会增加。
护肤品的市值在2014年至2019年间增长了约200亿美元，已成为化妆品行业利润最高的部门。产品可能非常昂贵，特别是当与其他产品组合成精心设计的养生法时。但汉布林和莱曼所描述的皮肤健康科学表明，当我们认为皮肤是静止的或独立的，应该通过表面涂抹各种洁面剂和保湿剂、粘液和粘液来护理时，我们就错了。(Hamblin对试图通过摩擦或摄取胶原蛋白来促进皮肤内部胶原生成的想法嗤之以鼻：“这就像你需要新轮胎，然后把橡胶放进油箱里一样。”)。从字面上讲，皮肤是一个生态系统，与我们身体其他部位的健康以及与外面的世界不断联系在一起。
在名为“皮肤旅行”的一章中，莱曼介绍了我们皮肤上的居住者。它们的范围从夜间在我们脸上游荡、交配的微小螨类，到生活在我们身体不同区域的高度稳定的微生物群落，每个微生物群落都有自己独特的环境条件。莱曼写道：“乍一看，我们的皮肤看起来像是一片光秃秃的、不适宜居住的风景。”事实上，对于足够小的生物来说，这里充满了山脊、峡谷、沙漠和沼泽：“充满野生动物的栖息地值得一部自然纪录片。“。这些栖息地反过来又受到我们自身环境条件的影响。在一项研究中，仅通过检查人们的皮肤微生物群，科学家就可以知道他们住在哪个城市，与谁同居。
如果阅读这篇文章让您想要使用更多的SOAP，而不是更少，我当然可以理解。(了解到所有这些微小的面螨，因为它们没有肛门，最终会死于它们在你脸上消耗的所有皮肤和油脂的备份，这可能无济于事。)。但请记住，皮肤微生物群一直伴随着我们，它包含的微生物数量至少与你全身细胞的数量一样多--可能是你全身细胞数量的三倍。它是你，以一种非常真实的方式，它服务于我们刚刚开始理解的目的。例如
note_en:
When my sister and I were young, we liked to come home from school and turn on “Guiding Light,” a soap opera on CBS. We only ever caught the last fifteen minutes of the hour-long show, but, because it wasn’t particularly subtle, this was plenty of time to follow even its most involved plotlines—such as when Reva Shayne, a nine-times-married character who had arcs as a talk-show host, a psychic, the princess of a fictional island, and a time traveller to the Civil War and Nazi Germany, had to fight Dolly, a devious clone that her most recent husband had made of her in order to spare her children from grief during the most recent of her presumed deaths.
  “Guiding Light” began, in 1937, as a radio show to promote a soap called Duz. (“Duz does everything.”) When it went off the air, in 2009, it was the longest-running show in broadcast history. It was owned, until the end, not by CBS but by Procter & Gamble, which began as a soap company and has been credited with inventing modern advertising in America. In addition to promoting its brands with paintings on trolley cars and billboards, the company developed more than twenty radio and television dramas. The first, “Oxydol’s Own Ma Perkins,” premièred in 1933; the last, “As The World Turns,” left the airwaves in 2010, by which time the term “soap opera” had become freestanding. You could even watch them, as I did, without ever knowing they had anything to do with a soap company.
  It was easy, until the  -19 outbreak, not to think very often or very deeply about soap. Early in the pandemic, this began to change. We learned which pop songs had choruses that we could sing to keep us scrubbing for a full twenty seconds; we learned that, at least during the pre-lockdown period, the lines outside men’s rooms grew suddenly longer—likely because (according to one study) only thirty-one per cent of men had previously been in the habit of washing their hands after using the bathroom. As distilleries and breweries pivoted to producing hand sanitizer, the  Times ran a piece explaining why old-fashioned soap was actually better at destroying the coronavirus: the hydrophobic tails of soap molecules bond with the lipid membrane that protects the virus, literally ripping it apart, while their hydrophilic heads bond with the water that washes the dead virus away. Like many people, I developed a new appreciation for soap, imagining with grim satisfaction a scene of microscopic destruction each time I scrubbed my hands.
 So this has been a strange time to be reading a book by a medical doctor which takes a critical view of the soap industry and begins with the sentence “Five years ago, I stopped showering.”
 Let me clarify at once that James Hamblin, a staff writer at  The Atlantic and the author of “ Clean: The New Science of Skin” (Riverhead), is still an advocate of regular handwashing, indisputably a world-changing innovation in public health, and of especially crucial importance at this moment in history. (Hamblin also writes that he “would never wear a white coat two days in a row without cleaning it.”) But he’s doubtful about all the scrubbing and soaping—not to mention moisturizing and deodorizing and serum-and-acid application—to which we subject the rest of our body’s largest organ, and about the companies that spend a lot of money to convince us that we must do so to be clean.
  Soap is an ancient invention, so old that we can only assume it was the lucky result of animal fat spilling into fire ash and some people being alert enough to notice the cleaning power of the resulting lather. Still, early versions, made with lye, could burn skin, and were used more often for laundry than for people. Bathing more commonly involved water, sand, pumice, scrapers, and oils or perfumes—though in certain places the whole notion was seen as dangerous. Some historical records suggest that washing was comparatively rare in the Western world: Marco Polo wrote of his surprise at how frequently people in India and China bathed, and Ahmad ibn Fadlan, who travelled from the court of Baghdad to the Volga River in the early tenth century, wrote that the people he met on his journey did not wash after eating, shitting, peeing, or having sex, and were “the filthiest of Allah’s creatures.” The French historian Jules Michelet described the European Middle Ages as “a thousand years without a bath.”
 In America, soap made for skin became commonly sold only in the nineteenth century, largely as a way to make money from the leftovers of the meatpacking industry, which produced large quantities of unused animal fat. Entrepreneurs added potash and made soap, for which they then needed to create public demand. These early “soapers” included William Procter and James Gamble, who began working together after marrying a pair of sisters; another familial pair, whose company name eventually changed from Lever Brothers to Unilever; and a man named William Wrigley, Jr., who gave away chewing gum as a promotion for his soap, but found that the gum was in higher demand.
 Last year, the beauty-and-personal-care market in the U.S. was valued at nearly a hundred billion dollars, which makes it hard to imagine a time when people had to be persuaded to use soap. But the soap industry, Hamblin argues, serves as an effective introduction to the history of American marketing. Early soap companies pioneered many techniques that we still see today: a single company owning lots of competing brands with nearly identical products, in order to foster feelings of consumer choice and loyalty; the use of “sponsored content,” such as the soap operas or Procter & Gamble’s “How to Bring Up a Baby,” which was part health pamphlet and part advertisement. The ad campaigns created a sense of lurking danger in the competition by claiming that their own products were safer and purer, or they promoted, as product virtues, obscure, jargony terms (“triple milled”) that consumers assumed to be important simply because they were touted on a package. The companies leaned, not at all subtly, on racism and classism to sell their products. They even used people who would now be called influencers, such as the film stars who appeared in “9 out of 10 screen stars use Lux Toilet Soap” ads. “Lever never even paid them,” Hamblin writes, “and the practice being so new, the stars apparently didn’t think to ask.”
 The other innovation was to create, and then meet, needs that people didn’t know they had. Hamblin notes that “B.O.” began as a marketing term, and that many soaps advertised as “antimicrobial” and “antibacterial” were less safe than standard soap, leaving behind dangerous compounds. (Many products that we now think of as soaps are actually detergents, made from synthetic compounds.)
 Meanwhile, soap companies, in order to expand their product lines, “had to sell the idea that soap was insufficient on its own—or that its effects had to be undone by yet more products,” Hamblin writes. You needed separate soaps for your hair, your body, your face, and even for different members of a family. (Albert Einstein, asked why he didn’t use shaving cream, then newly invented, is reported to have replied, “Two soaps? That is too complicated!”) To offset the drying effects of soap, you then needed other products—conditioners, moisturizers, toners. Hamblin identifies the 1957 introduction of Dove, whose cleaning power is reduced because it’s mixed with moisturizer, as the moment when the industry started moving toward selling a product that would do nothing at all.
  Hamblin’s decision to stop showering—he occasionally rinses—began as an experiment, as he started to question what he’d assumed to be the essentials of his life. He sold his car, cancelled his Internet, and, until his girlfriend objected, considered living in a van. But it also reflected a new understanding of how our skin functions: as a living, permeable “dynamic interface” that connects us to the world around us—“a complex, diverse ecosystem” rather than a barrier that should be sterile and pristine. Soap, he reasoned, washes away not only natural oils but also many of the microorganisms that make our skin their home, disrupting the natural balance they might otherwise achieve.
 Hamblin embarks on a rather disjointed and sometimes unsatisfying series of adventures meant to question what it means to be clean. He visits the Dr. Bronner’s Magic Soaps factory, gets a fancy facial, and attends the opening of a Glossier store and various product expos. One salesperson pours soap for men into shot glasses from what looks like a whiskey bottle; another tries to sell him a “fountain of youth stem cell moisturizer,” in which the stem cells come from pumpkins. In an effort to show how laxly we regulate the ingredients in cosmetics—fifteen hundred chemicals are banned or restricted in personal-care products in the E.U., compared with only eleven in the United States—he starts his own skin-care company. It’s called Brunson + Sterling (the motto: “Menscare for Fucking Perfect Skin”) and offers a mix of random ingredients in two-ounce bottles for two hundred dollars each. The product doesn’t sell, but it is legal.
  Most interestingly, Hamblin meets people who take a very different view of what skin care means. One woman afflicted with acne tried everything from scrubbing to antibiotics, Accutane, and hormonal birth control. (Caused in part by  Cutibacterium acnes, acne is one of the most common reasons for antibiotic prescriptions.) Things only got worse, until she gave up, stopped doing anything, and found her skin clearing. Sandy Skotnicki, a Canadian dermatologist, spends her winters begging itchy men to stop using shower gel and wonders if the twinned increases in over-washing and in eczema are more than just correlation. (Because Skotnicki’s patients want to be prescribed  something, Hamblin writes, she “has found a way to turn something into nothing,” by advocating regimented cleanses—by which she just means breaks from cleansing products.) Some scientists find that symptoms of eczema—which often involve an overabundance of  Staphylococcus aureus—can be treated with the application of a different bacteria.
  Hamblin interviews experts in immunology and microbiology who worry that, for some of us, humanity’s long-standing problem with hygiene has now reversed itself: instead of too little, we may have too much of it. These experts want us to think of hygiene more expansively—as a matter of health and balance, rather than one of sterility and purity. With all our soaps and sanitizers and antibiotics, in addition to so much time spent inside, away from dirt and animals and fresh air, we’ve created new problems for our immune systems, which miss out on the chance to encounter benign triggers and instead learn to overreact to perceived threats. Excess hygiene can also be a problem for the skin’s microbiome, which has an ecology that we’re just beginning to understand. Mark Holbreich, an allergist in Indiana, found that the Amish have notably low levels of allergies, eczema, and other skin problems, even when compared to genetically and culturally related groups, including Hutterites in South Dakota, whose children are generally raised farther from their farms. Julie Segre, who published the first maps of the bacterial and fungal diversity of human skin, notes that the recent fascination with probiotics in food doesn’t seem to have affected our thinking about skin health: “Everyone wants to eat Activia yogurt and colonize themselves with bacteria, and then they want to use Purell.” And Jack Gilbert, a microbiologist, greets Hamblin with a nonstandard hello: “So, I shower. I do shower, though I know the implications of it. Not every day, and I don’t tend to use a lot of soap when I do.”
 Medical textbooks are commonly illustrated with what are known as écorché figures—human anatomies of the type sketched by Leonardo da Vinci, their skin removed to better show the muscle and bone beneath. Monty Lyman, a dermatologist, believes that this reveals a failure of the medical establishment: regularly overlooking the medical importance of the skin, which serves as both wall and window between us humans and the outside world. He knows specialists in more glamorous fields who scoff at dermatology. (A surgeon friend tells him, grotesquely, that “the skin is the wrapping paper that covers the presents.”) In “ The Remarkable Life of the Skin” (Atlantic Monthly Press), Lyman’s goal is to push back: to teach readers to appreciate an organ that is often “invisible in plain sight. ”
 Skin is a strange little miracle. Were it removed, you would quickly lose the water in your body and die of dehydration. It protects you from deadly radiation and pathogens and helps you stay within the narrow temperature spectrum your body can tolerate, yet it is, at its thinnest, half the width of a penny. And the cells with which it faces the world are already effectively dead, and will, in general, last no longer than a month—a million or so are shed each day, filling your house with dust. As these skin cells are lost, they are replaced with new ones, taking their own self-sacrificial turn at the barricades to protect the trillions of other cells of which you are made. “Never was so much owed by so many to so few, ” Lyman writes.
 As is often true in medicine, the importance of skin is made most clear when its workings go awry. Lyman tells us about pellagra, a painful rash that was widespread in South Carolina in the early twentieth century, leading to “unstoppable diarrhea” and eventually psychosis, until it was finally cured by the introduction of a balanced diet; it is the reason that packaged bread now includes niacin. We’re introduced to children with xeroderma pigmentosum, a genetic condition that sabotages the natural repair system that searches for U.V. damage to DNA; they are sometimes known as “midnight children,” because of their need to avoid the sun, and develop skin cancer at horrifying rates. In epidermolysis bullosa, another genetic condition, no proteins connect the epidermis to the dermis, which means skin can be ripped off by “a shearing force as light as twisting a door handle.” A young patient with the condition, Hassan, had barely any skin remaining when he received a pioneering treatment: doctors harvested some of his skin cells, exposed them to a virus that carried a healthy version of the mutated gene, then used them to grow nine square feet of new skin in a lab, which they successfully grafted onto Hassan’s body.
 While Hamblin focusses on cleanliness, Lyman attempts a comprehensive look at skin, speeding through sections on touch, pain, the history of tattoos, the science of melanin, how different religions view nudity, and how our skin’s exposure to the sun affects our broader health. (Did you know that dogs and cats, perhaps because their fur blocks their skin’s ability to absorb sunlight and produce Vitamin D, secrete an oil that converts to Vitamin D when exposed to sunlight? It then has to be ingested orally, which is one reason that pets are always licking themselves.) The sun can affect the aging of skin even more profoundly than time itself does, Lyman notes. He writes about working in a clinic and wrongly assuming that a sixtysomething woman was the daughter, rather than the mother, of a fortysomething, sun-worshipping patient.
  Though he takes readers on a tour of fad skin treatments, from the “vampire facials” once favored by Kim Kardashian to Cleopatra’s daily bath in donkey milk, his only fully endorsed beauty treatments are sun protection, a healthy diet, and the avoidance of smoking, excessive drinking, and long-term stress. “It is no surprise that the 2008 financial crisis saw a record peak in psoriasis and eczema consultations,” Lyman writes. Disorders such as celiac disease, Crohn’s disease, rosacea, and eczema—all of which involve the skin, the immune system, and the gut—reveal just how intertwined these systems are. Eczema, for example, is a predictor of whether an infant will develop food allergies, and acne has been found to increase when a person’s diet becomes Westernized.
 Skin care, whose market value grew by some twenty billion dollars between 2014 and 2019, has become the most profitable sector of the cosmetics industry. Products can be wildly expensive, especially when combined with others into elaborate regimens. But the science of skin health, as described by Hamblin and Lyman, suggests that we err when we think of skin as static or as separate, to be ministered to by surface applications of various cleansers and moisturizers, goops and goos. (Hamblin scoffs at the idea of trying to promote skin’s internal collagen production by rubbing on, or ingesting, collagen: “It’s like if you needed new tires and you put rubber in your gas tank.”) Skin is, literally, an ecosystem, in constant connection with the health of the rest of our body, as well as with the world beyond.
 In a chapter called “Skin Safari,” Lyman gives a tour of the denizens of our skin. They range from the microscopic mites that wander around our faces at night, copulating, to the highly stable communities of microorganisms that live on the different regions of our bodies, each with its own unique environmental conditions. “At first glance, our skin looks like a bare, inhospitable landscape,” Lyman writes. In fact, for critters that are small enough, it’s full of ridges and canyons and deserts and swamps: “Habitats filled with wildlife worthy of a nature documentary. ” These habitats are affected, in turn, by our own environmental conditions. In one study, scientists could tell, just by examining people’s skin microbiome, what city they lived in and with whom they cohabitated.
  If reading this makes you want to use more soap, not less, I can certainly relate. (It probably won’t help to learn that all those tiny face mites, because they have no anuses, eventually die from the backup of all the skin and oil they have consumed on your face.) But bear in mind that the skin microbiome has always been with us, and includes at least as many microorganisms as you have cells in your entire body—maybe three times as many. It is you, in a very real way, and it serves purposes we’re only beginning to understand. For example, microbes called archaea, discovered on skin in 2017, may be caretakers of our skin, helping it turn over nitrogen and keep pathogens at bay; smelly microbes on our feet may discourage fungal infections; and even those face mites could be thought of as natural exfoliants. One person’s skin is home to “a thousand species of bacterium, not to mention fungi, viruses, and mites,” Lyman writes—a diversity of characters and story lines that would put any soap opera to shame, and that have real outcomes for our health and our well-being. He tells of a smelly person who, after being swabbed with microbes taken from the armpits of his sweet-smelling twin, stopped stinking, and another who used dietary changes to combat a socially crippling genetic condition known, evocatively, as “fish-odor syndrome.” Lyman expects that science will soon be able to alter our unique microbial selves in ways that are far more sophisticated than using, or giving up, soap: “Manipulating and adjusting these populations has the potential to revolutionize medicine.”
 Hamblin, too, learns that soap is a small player compared with other things that affect the microbiome: use of antibiotics, say, or the early life experiences that affect its initial development. Rethinking soap, he decides, may be most important as a symbol of the way that we think about cleanliness: Is it a war or a balancing act? He concludes his book with an ode to public parks as a key part of urban hygiene, wishing that we spent more of the money that we’ve shelled out for soaps and skin care on improving our own habitats—both those that we live in and those that we are. “A
